speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three for Abe Danko, August 6, 1995. OK, you were telling about Crawinkel and that it was an underground camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so talk about that a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were coming over there. There was no window no nothing, just under a hill, and we were coming in maybe about 500 guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '45. And over there was airplanes, cars, everything, and we supposed to clean it. Take this and put it over there. They show it to you what to do and put everything. We did what we can do, whatever they tell us to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Food was no problem. They don't have, and they don't give. That's what they told you right away. We don't have and we don't give, we don't have none. We tried to organize, you know. But what are you going to organize? If it's a German he got it, he open his-- he say lunchtime, he got there our bags. He opens, he got a sandwich. A big sandwich was as big as a finger. And he make a big [SPEAKING YIDDISH] if he give you-- he broked our piece. I got a hole in my tooth. The sandwich would go in, and I wouldn't even fill up the tooth. So you're saying that the Germans didn't have food either.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe they're not used to food. But they talk about food in Hirschberg, and I was in Hirschberg. He was so hungry, only was potatoes outside the camp under the ground and cover it up with straw on top. You know a test one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we was under electric wires. It was three guys, and we took a risk. In the middle of the night we dig it, dig it, dig it, dig it, till we dig a big hole, and it was going out to the potatoes. We took in six potatoes. We dig it with bare hands with our spoon. You know a spoon what you got to eat? And we digged out six potatoes, and we come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: The potatoes what was left we left it up which they going to freeze, and we come in and-- we know this when we want to do something in this one so we're not going to catch us. So we took the potato to the job. And over there I was working, I told you, in a [NON-ENGLISH], and over there was only hot and you just put it for 10 minutes. After you go in you got a baked potato. But they caught the potato crate is open.

speaker: interviewer
text: They'll hold us 24 hours. Everybody outside and the biggest frost. Somebody must come out and say who did this. Nobody goes out. People falling apart in fear this one, nobody. Who going to go out?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the potatoes in Hirschberg. Now going back to Crawinkel, we come into Crawinkel, and they told you right away, we don't have food, and we don't serve, unless they going to send us. Meanwhile we worked. I think the general was not anxious to finish the job either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what we did we did. We was maybe about two or three weeks over there, and there's only going to go out from here. Where we're going, what are we going, they don't know. And we start marching.

speaker: interviewee
text: We going from Crawinkel maybe about two or three weeks walking. We didn't know where, and what, and why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they let you rest at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is not such a thing, a rest at night, but they would have rest and say, OK, stay. And after 10, 15 minutes they say, let's go. We by the end of this one, we go to the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you marched for three weeks--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and after then we come to our train. We come to our train, and they put us in. Maybe it was about 5,000 or 10,000 people. Of course, they come from all over and all around us were walking people coming in. And they put us in a wagon. Not a [NON-ENGLISH] wagon. On this one maybe about 501. And every 15, 20 minutes they stopped. You go down, you go down. People start looking for food, you're on the grass cooking, and there's cooking, and there's making. They wouldn't help nothing. Up and down, up and down. They sure to tell you the story we wind up in Theresienstadt. We come to Theresienstadt. We was so hungry. Theresienstadt was not a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresienstadt was like a ghetto or an old age home. I don't know. It was no camp because over there you can see people. A 70-year-old people can you see. You can see a small child from two years. And food, they have rations, but you talk to so many people about the rations. They're looking to organize, and they found where the food is. And our whole night was eating and cooking. Tomorrow morning, it's an appeal, togetherness, and they told us a story. What you did, even a bad German wouldn't do this because you destroyed for four weeks what we have for four weeks prepared for all the old people and all the children. You destroyed that, and you took it away. You almost killed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can talk to the wall. Nobody answer nothing. If you're hungry you are you going to do anything to kill your hunger. And we were over there approximately three or four weeks. And we opened the window. It was call up downstairs to the-- was watching this one. We opened the window, or we going to close the window?

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day we want to open the window, and he answer you can or you don't. Nobody answer. We call again, nobody answer. If you seeing this, people are running. They're saying the war is over. So the war is over. We go down.

speaker: interviewer
text: We go down, and people said to me, it's stupid. Where you going? Let's go to the basement. The basement, there's bread, and there's [NON-ENGLISH], and there's everything over there. We go down-- there was really blocks of-- fat, so big, big blocks, bread and everything. And we take it, and we start eating, and instead of eat bread in butter, we eat butter in bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the evening everybody's on typhus. The Russian they liberated us, and we all in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Typhus, everybody got typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the hospital, there in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Theresienstadt, yeah. And we was over there till the crisis-- a lot had died. A lot, a lot had died. When the crisis passed they start giving us goulash, three times a day, meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can tell you maybe I weighed 90 pounds and when I got finished with a-- I was four weeks over there-- maybe I got 120, 130 already. Had three times a day how much meat you want. Just brought the kessel, they put it on the plate. You want any more? Yeah, give you more. So you ate a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: [WHISTLES] So we want to go home. He say you can go home. So how can you go home without bringing something for your mother and for your father, something, a present?

speaker: interviewer
text: You thought when you got home everybody would be there? We didn't know that. And that's the reason we didn't know. So we was going, organizing, and we going into [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, [NON-ENGLISH] is a warehouse. And I took a package of a dozen-- and you wear under the dress, a slips. Oh. Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what I'm talking-- under your dress you wear a slip?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: A half slip. I took a half slip and said, OK, it's going to be for mother. And the train was free. And I go to Poland. And I come to Poland. I run fast to where I live. I don't see nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did go back to Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, I see nobody, and I don't see any Jews. I don't know what to do, and there it's already 6 o'clock in the evening. And I ask where are the Jews? He said, oh, you're looking for Jews. Go on this street. Maybe you're going to find Jews. And over there was, you know the mikveh and the [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what a mikveh is-- a guest, a Jewish guest coming to sleep overnight who didn't have any money, poor guys sleeping overnight. I come over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: A guy said to me, first of all grab a bed because if you don't grab a bed you don't have it to sleep. OK, I grabbed a bed. He tells me a story that's he's already the third night here. I say, how long can you stay here? He said, I can stay all my life here. Who cares? I say, where do you eat? I said, where are you? I said, I'm born-- I'm from here. Are you stupid? You don't tell me stories. You tell me who are you. I say, do you think of what I have to lie. What is to lie here? I'm from here. You are not. You cannot be because if you would be from here you wouldn't sleep here. So where I would sleep and I tell him the story. He said, why, you already in the [NON-ENGLISH]? I said, what's a [NON-ENGLISH]? Oh you're a green horn, and you don't know nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he tells me a story that's on this and this street is a [NON-ENGLISH], and they give you money and then some. OK, tomorrow morning I'm going to go. I come over there, and I stay and wait and a guy takes me in. And the same thing I was with this guy in school together. But he was maybe about three years older. He was three classes ahead of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: In public school or in cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: In public school. And he say, I don't know if you are really [SPEAKING YIDDISH] from this city, but I take your word for it. I said, don't take my word for it. I can tell you where you store was. I can tell you where your father is. And I'll tell you. He said, OK, don't tell me so much. He takes out 500 zloty and give me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I was thinking because he gives me a lot of money. And I picked myself up. And I was hungry when I got to the restaurant. And I ate of a goose. You know a goose? I ate a supper. And I come to pay. I asked him how much I owe. And he said, 500 zloty. I say, if you would say 501, I wouldn't pay you nothing. Because that's what I just got there from the [NON-ENGLISH]. He said, OK, you go tomorrow again. I said, you bet your life I will go again.

speaker: interviewer
text: I come tomorrow again. And the same guy takes me says, what are you doing here? I said, what do you mean what I'm doing here? You give me yesterday 500 zloties. Sure, I gave it to you. But so I thought I was going to the restaurant to eat the supper, and they told me to pay 500 zloties. Who told you to go to the restaurant? So where I should go that's not my business. That's your business. OK, I give you another five, don't pull any more. He said, I'm going to come as long as you know because I don't know where to go. You tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But going down from over there I found a cousin. A cousin which he is here and one is in Israel. And he tells me, oh, fine, well, come on let's go home. He takes me home. There were two brothers and a cousin. This one. And they have two brothers. The two cousins got their wives, and one is a single. OK. Very good, they got two rooms, a kitchen, and a room. And by talking to us he said, Abe, you can every thing you can have with what is here. But the one thing you cannot sleep here. I said, oh wait a minute. I forgot something. And I was going down and never showed up again to them. Never showed up again. The single boy, he understood me. And after three days he found me in the [SPEAKING YIDDISH], in the guest house. And he said to me, I know why you're angry, but believe me if you would go in their situation, you wouldn't be angry. There are two couples. There are two beds. I sleep on the floor. And you know the couples what they're doing in the night and I'm not [INAUDIBLE]. So they don't want to take you in because that's what this life. What we can do I wouldn't go back to them either. Let's go, let's take the rooms, and let's make something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's make a kibbutz like something. And there was a guest from Katowice, from another city, and he was working with the Bricha. It's just like a go to Israeli. He was working sending illegal people to Israel. We go to him, and he said, OK, we will provide you money and food. And you organize here. You're going to have more people coming in, give us beds, [INAUDIBLE], straw sacks, you know, and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the night they come to our car, you know, and they shoot at us. A car with the anti-Semite device.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, or an organization called [NON-ENGLISH]. And this is anti-Semite. And we start complaining, and we called him up. And he said, don't worry you're headed to Denmark and that's what. In there in that spatial across the street where we was in-- they rented two rooms. And they put four soldiers, Jewish soldiers that was in the Army. And they put them up stairs. And if they start shooting. They're going to shoot on them, and they're going to come down to something. And that's what was. And they start shooting in the night. They shoot back. And they didn't know that there was soldiers. They going up there. And they come up. They saw the soldiers and said, if you come once more you're not going to be out alive here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What you want from them? They just was liberated. What do you want?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meanwhile, a Gentile come to me, and he say he wants to buy the house. I say, give me money. He said, no it doesn't work this way. He say, you come with me to a lawyer, and the lawyer going to take it over because it's not yours. It's just your parents. And if you going to settle, you're going to have money. And I knew that if you go to a lawyer it cost money just for talking to him. So I just knock on the door, he comes out, he say, listen man. I don't have one penny to give it to you. This guy wants to buy my father's house. If you could take it, for him money take it I don't have nothing. He said, you come in. OK. I come in and was on table glass with cigarettes. Maybe about 10, 12 cigarettes. I asked him if I can take a cigarette. He said, OK. I already took three, two in pocket, and one in mouth. Do you know when I walked out from him I got all the cigarettes, and he give me 1,000 zloties, the lawyer. He said, you don't worry. It's your money. I just what I need is a witness that's your parents were killed and he saw it and your brothers were killed that he saw it. I just need witnesses. I said, I got witnesses. I got the cousins. He said, that's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was like a judge that was going to them. And he told them. They put the house on my name. And that cost 10,000 zloty to take it over from one to another. I said, I don't have money. He say, who told you about money? He pays everything. The lawyer pays everything. And after then the customer says, how much you want it? I said, I don't know. You tell me. He say, I tell you the truth. The Russian is here. In a big house the Russian going to nationalize. So how much you can take it out, take it out. So he said take a quarter of a million zloty. What I know? You said, so and so and so. OK. Until I get it, the money, I got 120,000. He took it, but 30,000 for his job, and he paid there 10,000, to pay this one. I don't know where it was I got 120,000.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the 120,000 I make myself a pair of boots, and I bought myself a pair of pants and a sweater, and I bought for my cousin the same thing. And it cost me $50,000. 60. sexy. You make a big party in the kibbutz and all the money is gone for the kibbutz. What I've got left only is the shoes with the pants with the sweater. But I didn't come with nothing, I go with nothing. And we was going to Germany. We were supposed to go to Israel illegally. So they gave me-- I was there in this time as I really was going as Greek Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Greek Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: As Greek Jews back to home. And they gave me a passport, I was 16 years old. And you're my girlfriend, but you are 32. And you are Greek too. And we supposed to talk only from siddur. You know from the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Only Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No Hebrew. [NON-ENGLISH] just from siddur. Because Jewish they wouldn't understand, Polish they understand. Russian they didn't understand, whatever you talk about. He said, just talk from siddur. [NON-ENGLISH] We just talk from siddur. And if I saw he wants a cigarette I say, [NON-ENGLISH] So I give him a cigarette. When we come to [NON-ENGLISH] said this, it's a Polish-Czechish border, and the Russian took us in to check. And we was going out, the general said to me, Shalom. And I look at him, and I am going back and I go out. And we're going out we come to Czechoslovakia. The Russian and this one didn't want to go away. They wanted the girls. And every boy was responsible for a girl. So wherever they're going, you will run after them. Everybody was running after them, and we winded it up in [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. And we cannot do nothing. And I was aggravated with him-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the leader. And he tells me, if you don't like it you will stay here. You will not go no place.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said if I don't go no place go no place. So he said to me, you know what? You're going to be the last one to go. I said, oh no, when the first one goes, I got the second. But when I saw the-- that was a tale I told you. When I saw the rucksack, you know what you carry on their backs. And the first one the girl said, that's really the only. I said, oh, no. Go away. There she grab my boot, and he throw me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my foot got swollen, so I was always the last one because I cannot walk. So until I walk to the train the train is already gone with the people. And I with the girl, stayed. I don't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I ask the people over there, there is no other transportation? They say, oh yeah, there is. There is buses. We come to the buses in the hundreds, people. We want to go to Asch. It is the last city from the border of Germany. And the driver is on top on the bus, and he loads the luggage. And I don't know. I don't have any money. And I ask in Polish, how much cost to go to Asch? And he ask me, who are you? Why am I going to lie to him? I said, I'm a Jew. A Jew? What are you going to do in Asch?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, I'm wondering until I find a place where I can make a living. He goes down. And he grabs two Germans, and he throws them out. Come on, sit down, to the girl and me. We sit down. I said, wait a minute, you know, I don't have any money. And she said, maybe he going to accept cigarettes. And cigarettes I got.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cigarettes, they could kill for a cigarette. And I said, are you smoker? [WHISTLES] He grabs. I say, you know what? I don't have any money. I give you the pack of cigarettes for us to go to Asch. Good, good. Who knows where Asch is? He drove maybe a half hour. He said, that's it, Ash. I go down. So where you going? I don't know. I said to her, you know what? I'm going to try to put down my boots and ask where the station is, the train station, maybe I'll find somebody. You sit here in park, and I'm going to see. I put down, and I come to the train station. I found a guy. He said, how do you come here? I told you the story of how I come. He said, no you cannot go away because the train is supposed to come in another 15 minutes. So I was before the train even there. And before we left the kibbutz give us money to hide everybody when we come to the next stop, where we have some money to make a living to live. So I say, you stay here. I go to the girl, and I'm going to tell her that she can wait or I bring her here. He said, bring her here. We go together. I brought her over there. And everybody would come down. Everybody said that they took it away everything. They took away everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know this is not true because by me they took no way, nothing because I was not in the train. And by the girl either. But I know my cousin. He hide their money. He got the bread, and he cut it out the bread. And he put in the money and stick up the bread together there. And I saw the way he holds the bread. If they took away the money, he wouldn't hold the bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I let go. And I said to him, I know this. You're bullshitting because it's not true. He said, listen. Some is true. Some is not, but don't say nothing. Everybody says so. Say it like this. So what I'm going to say? You don't have a choice. You must help out because it was not in this. So what are you going to do? I say, with mine we going to divide. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I get the money, we go in and we're going into a restaurant and we ask for food. He say, no. Only [NON-ENGLISH] coupons. Listen, we got American dollar, and we want to have some food. And we not particular what kind of food. Whatever you give, just give us. What he say he will come 12 o'clock in the night. We're going to prepare for you potatoes and [NON-ENGLISH], like you make macarons but they say balls like this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, we're going to stop now.
